question: What does it mean for a polynomial in n variables to be multilinear, and how many coefficients does the space of multilinear polynomials in n variables have?
answer_key: Multilinear means individual degree at most 1 in each variable separately, so every monomial is a product of a distinct subset of the n variables (e.g. 1, x‚āĀ, x‚āā, x‚āĀx‚āā, ‚Ä¶). There is one coefficient per subset of the n variables, so the space has 2‚ĀŅ coefficients (dimension 2‚ĀŅ). Note this is NOT total degree 1 ‚ÄĒ terms like x‚āĀx‚āāx‚āÉ are allowed, so the total degree can be as high as n.
targets_stuck_point: 'Multilinear means total degree 1 (a flat plane)' ‚ÄĒ individual degree ‚Č§ 1 per variable still allows products like x‚āĀx‚āāx‚āÉ.
on_miss_route_to: multivariate polynomials; total degree vs individual (per-variable) degree

question: Let f:{0,1}¬≥‚ÜíF_97 have cube values f(000..111) = [1,4,2,8,3,5,7,6]. Using MLE(x)=ő£_w f(w)¬∑‚ąŹŠĶĘ(xŠĶĘwŠĶĘ+(1‚ąíxŠĶĘ)(1‚ąíwŠĶĘ)), what is fŐÉ(2,5,3) over F_97, and does fŐÉ reproduce f on the corners?
answer_key: fŐÉ(2,5,3) = 5 over F_97. Each basis term ‚ąŹŠĶĘ(xŠĶĘwŠĶĘ+(1‚ąíxŠĶĘ)(1‚ąíwŠĶĘ)) is 1 at corner w and 0 at every other corner, so on any corner x=w the sum collapses to f(w) ‚ÄĒ fŐÉ agrees with f at all 8 corners. At the off-cube point (2,5,3) the basis terms are no longer 0/1; the weighted sum evaluates to 5 (confirmed independently by building fŐÉ as a polynomial over GF(97) and evaluating).
targets_stuck_point: 'The MLE is only defined on the cube' ‚ÄĒ (2,5,3) is off the cube yet fŐÉ has a genuine value there.
on_miss_route_to: univariate Lagrange interpolation and the low-degree extension (LDE)

question: A verifier holds a table of 2‚ĀŅ values and wants to reduce a claim about the whole table to a claim about one polynomial it can probe at a random point. Why is the MLE the right object, and why multilinear rather than a single univariate degree-(2‚ĀŅ‚ąí1) interpolant?
answer_key: The MLE is the unique low-degree polynomial that encodes the table: it agrees with the table on the cube and is defined everywhere, so a random-point query (via Schwartz‚ÄďZippel) tests the whole table at once. Multilinear (degree 1 in each of n variables) is chosen over a univariate degree-(2‚ĀŅ‚ąí1) interpolant because the per-variable structure lets a verifier strip ONE variable per round ‚ÄĒ this is exactly what sum-check (Ch 8) does, reducing an n-variable claim to an (n‚ąí1)-variable claim, down to a single evaluation. A single high-degree univariate object has no such coordinate-by-coordinate handle. (This same MLE structure is what makes Lasso/Jolt lookup tables evaluable in O(log N) without committing to them.)
targets_stuck_point: 'Use univariate degree-(2‚ĀŅ‚ąí1) interpolation instead' ‚ÄĒ flattening to one variable destroys the per-coordinate structure sum-check exploits.
on_miss_route_to: the Boolean hypercube {0,1}‚ĀŅ as an index set

question: You could have invented this. You have a function f given only on the 2‚ĀŅ corners of {0,1}‚ĀŅ and you want ONE polynomial that (a) reproduces f on the corners and (b) is as simple as possible. Derive the extension and argue it is unique ‚ÄĒ what basis do you build, and why is there exactly one answer?
answer_key: For one variable, the indicator 'x equals w' is x when w=1 and (1‚ąíx) when w=0 ‚ÄĒ i.e. xw+(1‚ąíx)(1‚ąíw). Take products across the n coordinates: Ōá_w(x)=‚ąŹŠĶĘ(xŠĶĘwŠĶĘ+(1‚ąíxŠĶĘ)(1‚ąíwŠĶĘ)) is 1 at corner w and 0 at every other corner. Then fŐÉ(x)=ő£_w f(w)¬∑Ōá_w(x) reproduces f on the cube by construction, and each Ōá_w is multilinear, so fŐÉ is multilinear. Uniqueness: the multilinear polynomials in n variables form a 2‚ĀŅ-dimensional space, and the 2‚ĀŅ corner-evaluation constraints form an invertible system (the Ōá_w are a basis), so exactly one multilinear polynomial agrees with f on the cube. You have rediscovered the multilinear extension ‚ÄĒ the multivariate echo of Lagrange interpolation.
targets_stuck_point: 'The extension is non-unique / arbitrary' ‚ÄĒ restricting to multilinear (2‚ĀŅ coefficients, 2‚ĀŅ constraints) pins down exactly one.
on_miss_route_to: the idea of a vector space over a field (a basis spans uniquely)
